The Browser Render Pipeline Every Frontend Engineer Should Know

A student asked me about this topic recently, and I think it's actually one of the more important things frontend engineers should understand. We work with browsers constantly — not knowing how they work is leaving knowledge on the table.
By which I mean: the browser's internals are way more complicated than they look. The simple mental model goes something like:
User requests an HTML file from the server.
Browser combines HTML, CSS, JS, and images into a page.
That's a fine simplification, but the actual rendering and execution flow is way more complex.
If you're a frontend engineer, you've heard a thousand times that JavaScript is single-threaded. And you might've assumed: since the browser runs JavaScript, the browser must also be single-threaded?
Wrong. The browser is actually multi-threaded under the hood. That's why JavaScript has async — the browser offloads certain work to other threads, and notifies the main thread when it's done.
Why bring this up? Because the browser's internals roughly split into five process types:
Quick note: Network Process and GPU Process were split out from the Browser Process because they're time-consuming. Separating them lets the Browser Process focus on other work.
Today's focus is Render Process, but it's worth briefly covering what the others do:
: handles network requests — downloading HTML, CSS, JS, images, etc. — and hands the results back to the Browser Process.
: handles GPU work, e.g. compositing the page onto the screen. It takes Render Process output and passes it to the GPU, which uses hardware acceleration to improve performance.
: handles legacy plugin modules (Flash, Java Applet, etc.). Modern browsers barely use plugins anymore, but some architectures still keep this process around for compatibility.
When you request a page, the browser does a series of steps:
: parse the HTML response. This typically contains HTML tags, CSS, JS, image tags, etc.
: convert parsed HTML into the DOM tree — the Document Object Model — a tree structure containing all HTML elements and attributes.
: parse the CSS response into the CSSOM (CSS Object Model).
: combine DOM tree and CSSOM into a Render Tree.
: compute the size and position of each node in the Render Tree.
: paint each node in the Render Tree.
: compose the different layers into the final image. This stage uses the GPU for acceleration — everything you see on screen is actually layers stacked together.
: run JS code, which can modify the DOM tree and CSSOM.
Static assets → DOM tree → CSSOM → Render Tree → Layout → Page Paint → Composite
Now: every time the DOM or CSS changes, two operations potentially run —
: recompute size and position of each node in the DOM tree.
: redraw elements affected by style changes (e.g. background color, border, shadow, color) — but doesn't recompute layout.
Anything that affects DOM structure or style can trigger Reflow and Repaint. And when triggered, the whole pipeline runs again from the top. That's why DOM operations are expensive.
In general: changing DOM structure triggers both Reflow and Repaint. Changing only styles or attributes triggers just Repaint. For performance, you want to be careful with DOM manipulation.
Finally, Virtual DOM. As a frontend engineer, you'll definitely run into frameworks, and these frameworks use Virtual DOM for performance and developer ergonomics.

The core idea: when you "modify the DOM", you're actually modifying a virtual representation first, and the framework batches changes before applying them to the real DOM — not applying them one by one immediately. This reduces the cost of DOM operations.
So… does Virtual DOM make things faster?

Honestly, no. It adds some computational overhead — Virtual DOM converts the DOM into JavaScript objects, and when you update, it first updates the virtual representation, diffs against the real DOM, then patches the differences in. The reason Virtual DOM feels faster is because of the optimizations baked into it — Diff Algorithm, Batch Update, etc. — which reduce real DOM operations. But Virtual DOM in itself isn't faster than the real DOM — it just makes DOM manipulation easier and safer.
